reforms.description: The Norwegian government proposed a reform to allow workers to spend one-tenth of their normal working time on education, aimed at improving job-related training access for lower-skilled workers.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: Improving access to education for lower-skilled workers enhances human capital, which is essential for productivity and innovation.
reforms.status_evidence: The reform has been proposed but not yet implemented.
reforms.importance_rationale: The reform significantly impacts workforce skills but is not a structural overhaul.
reforms.source_quote: The main federation of labour unions in Norway, the LO, has recently made the proposal, perhaps inspired by a Danish reform, to allow workers to spend one-tenth of their normal working time on education.

reforms.description: Norway is implementing reforms to improve research links between the public and private sectors, including the Public R&D Contracts Scheme (OFU) and the Industrial R&D Contracts Scheme (IFU), aimed at enhancing collaboration between public research and private industry.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: Enhancing collaboration between public research and private industry facilitates technology diffusion and innovation.
reforms.status_evidence: The schemes have been put into practice to enhance joint public/private research.
reforms.importance_rationale: The reforms significantly improve collaboration but do not represent a complete overhaul of the system.
reforms.source_quote: The SND has implemented two schemes to increase joint public/private research in the development of advanced technology: the Public R&D Contracts Scheme (OFU) and the Industrial R&D Contracts Scheme (IFU).

reforms.description: The government is implementing three technology-based programmes for small firms: Innovation and Entrepreneurship (FORNY), Regional Development of SMEs (RUSH), and Technology Diffusion from Research Institutes (TEFT), aimed at promoting innovative start-ups and enhancing collaboration between research and industry.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: Supporting innovative start-ups and enhancing collaboration fosters entrepreneurship and economic growth.
reforms.status_evidence: The programmes have been actively implemented to support small firms.
reforms.importance_rationale: The implementation of multiple significant programmes represents a structural change in support for SMEs.
reforms.source_quote: The government is taking an activist stance to technology policy. The Research Council of Norway (NFR) implements three technology-based programmes for small firms: Innovation and Entrepreneurship (FORNY), Regional Development of SMEs (RUSH) and Technology Diffusion from Research Institutes (TEFT).

reforms.description: Norway reformed its competition policy significantly in January 1994, shifting from an 'abuse' principle to a 'prohibition' principle, making various anti-competitive practices illegal and enhancing competition in goods markets.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: Enhancing competition in goods markets fosters innovation and efficiency.
reforms.status_evidence: The reform has been in force since January 1994.
reforms.importance_rationale: The shift in competition policy represents a major structural change in the regulatory environment.
reforms.source_quote: Competition policy in Norway was reformed significantly in January 1994 – upon entry into the EEA – when the new competition law replaced the Price Act of 1953.
